text: WHO Safe Abortion Care and Post-Abortion Care Guidance
Introduction and Scope

Safe abortion care includes: (1) accurate diagnosis and provision of accurate information, (2) assessment of eligibility and safety of different methods, (3) provision of highly effective and safe methods of abortion, (4) management of complications, and (5) provision of post-abortion contraception and related reproductive health services.
Medical Abortion (Medication-Induced)

Medical abortion uses medications to terminate pregnancy. The regimen most commonly recommended is mifepristone (200 mg) followed by misoprostol (400-800 μg) given 36-48 hours later. Success rates range from 94-98% depending on gestation and dose.
Medical Abortion Protocols by Gestation

caption: Medical Abortion Regimens by Gestational Age

text: Mifepristone works by blocking progesterone receptors, causing decidualization and detachment of trophoblastic tissue from the uterus. Misoprostol causes uterine contractions and cervical softening, expelling the pregnancy.
Medical Abortion Follow-up

Follow-up is essential to confirm complete abortion and rule out ectopic pregnancy. Follow-up assessment at 7-14 days includes: (1) clinical examination, (2) pelvic ultrasound or urine/serum hCG testing to confirm complete abortion.
If abortion is incomplete (10-15% of cases), options include: (1) expectant management (wait and observe), (2) repeat misoprostol dose, or (3) surgical completion (aspiration or curettage).
Surgical Abortion

Surgical abortion includes vacuum aspiration (manual or electric suction) and sharp curettage. Vacuum aspiration is the safest and most effective method when performed correctly. Curettage is useful for retained products.
Vacuum Aspiration Technique

items: Perform bimanual examination to assess uterine size and position
Prepare instruments: speculum, tenaculum, cannula (size 6-12 mm depending on gestation), syringe or electric pump
Grasp cervix with tenaculum and gently straighten uterus
Insert local anesthetic (paracervical block with 1% lidocaine) or use misoprostol for cervical priming
Dilate cervix gradually using dilators; stop when resistance met (no forcing needed)
Insert suction cannula gently to fundus
Apply suction gradually (vacuum 0.4-0.6 kg/cm²) and use curette or rotation to ensure complete evacuation
Stop when uterus feels empty (tissue no longer obtained in cannula)
Check for completeness with curettage or ultrasound

text: Management of Abortion Complications
Hemorrhage/Heavy Bleeding
Heavy bleeding after abortion may indicate incomplete abortion, infection, or uterine perforation. Management depends on severity and cause.

caption: Management of Post-Abortion Hemorrhage
rows: Observe; may be normal; provide IV fluids if dehydrated
>2 pads/hour or sustained passage of clots
IV access; blood type and cross-match; oxytocin 10 units IV; ergot 0.5 mg IM; misoprostol 600-800 μg PV
Large bore IV access; type O blood; obstetric team; prepare for OR; avoid hysterectomy if possible; uterine massage; oxytocin infusion; consider tranexamic acid 1 g IV

text: Infection/Sepsis

Fever >38°C post-abortion suggests infection. Risk factors include unsterile procedure, incomplete abortion, and retained products. Treat with broad-spectrum antibiotics and evacuation of uterus if incomplete.
Post-Abortion Contraception

Contraception should be offered to all women undergoing abortion. Most contraceptive methods can be initiated immediately post-abortion. IUD insertion is safe within 48 hours of second-trimester abortion or immediately after first-trimester abortion.

caption: Contraception Timing Post-Abortion
rows: Start same day; no increased risks
Within 48 hours (first trimester); immediately after second trimester
Optimal: within 48 hours; can insert later but schedule must be reliable
Can use alone or with other methods
Do not perform immediately due to risk of regret; defer unless woman is absolutely certain

text: Counselling and Informed Consent

Pre-abortion counselling should include: (1) information about the procedure and expected course, (2) possible complications and how to seek care, (3) emotional support and referral to counselling if needed, (4) contraception options and planning, and (5) follow-up care arrangements.
